title: Dog Sleeping Significantly More Than Usual: Causes & Triage Advice
meta_desc: Is your dog sleeping more than usual? Learn when lethargy is a concern, how to monitor your pet, and when to seek veterinary advice.
content_html:
TL;DR: While dogs naturally sleep 12-14 hours a day, a significant increase in sleep or lethargy can signal underlying health issues ranging from fatigue to chronic conditions. It is usually not an emergency if they are still eating and drinking, but you should monitor them closely and record videos for your veterinarian.

What does it mean if my dog is sleeping more than usual?

Increased sleep, often referred to as lethargy, occurs when a dog lacks their normal energy levels or enthusiasm for typical activities like walking or playing. While it is normal for dogs to sleep between 12 to 14 hours a day, a significant shift in their baseline behavior can indicate underlying issues ranging from minor fatigue or aging to more serious chronic health conditions.

Is my dog's increased sleep or lethargy considered an emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Low. In most cases where a dog is simply sleeping more but is still eating, drinking, and moving normally when awake, this is not an immediate emergency.
  • Monitor your pet closely for any secondary symptoms such as loss of appetite, vomiting, or a reluctance to move.
  • If your dog remains responsive and alert when prompted, a scheduled appointment with your veterinarian is the appropriate next step.

How can capturing a photo or video of my dog help with veterinary triage?

  • Capture a video of your dog while they are resting or showing their movement when they finally wake up.
  • Visual evidence allows a professional to observe your dog's respiratory rate, resting posture, and gait.
  • Video helps differentiate between simple tiredness and signs of physical pain or neurological issues that may not be obvious during a physical exam at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, increased sleeping or lethargy may be attributed to a variety of underlying medical conditions. These include metabolic disorders such as hypothyroidism or Cushing's disease, pain, sensory decline, diseases affecting the central nervous system (e.g., tumors), or circulatory issues such as anemia or hypertension. Furthermore, cognitive dysfunction syndrome (CDS) should be considered, particularly in geriatric dogs. True exercise intolerance due to respiratory failure can also manifest as lethargy, although unwillingness to exercise is more commonly due to orthopedic disease or obesity. A thorough history, physical and neurologic examination, and appropriate diagnostic tests are essential to determine the underlying cause of the increased sleeping.

Chapter: Neurology, Geriatrics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1569)

faqs.question: How much sleep is considered normal for an adult dog?
faqs.answer: Most healthy adult dogs sleep between 12 and 14 hours per day, though this can increase based on age, size, and activity level.

faqs.question: Can weather or season change affect my dog's sleep?
faqs.answer: Yes, very hot weather can cause dogs to be less active and sleep more to conserve energy and stay cool.

faqs.question: When does lethargy become an emergency?
faqs.answer: If your dog is 'obtunded' (difficult to wake up), has pale gums, or is struggling to breathe, you should seek emergency veterinary care immediately.
